entry.title: Re: Next Phase of the New Table Build Experience: Switching Existing Stories
entry.content:
Hi @selti,

thanks for your question. When you are creating new stories in optimized design AND when you are migrating from classic to optimized, we will be using the new table experience automatically. 

During the migration from classic to optimized, the table will be migrated to the new table experience. When you create a new optimized story, the new table experience will be used automatically.

In both situatiions you should see a quick message in the builder panel confirming that you are indeed in the new table experience:

 

If you are observing different behavior, please let me know and create a ticket with SAP Support.

Best regards,
Anna

entry.title: Re: Worklist not seen in Process collection Worklist App
entry.content:
Good day @Sandeep_SubbarajuYarapa 

Thank you for your question.

If the dispute case is set to Status 23, the item should become relevant for collections, but it will only appear in the Process Collection Worklist after the required collection worklist generation process has run successfully. You can find detail of this in KBA 3711867, it explains the case where no items appear in Manage Collection Worklist even after you have completed the BFC configuration.

In simple terms, please check the following:

  • You must assign the customer to a collection profile/collection segment.
  • The open item must be relevant for collections.
  • You must linke the dispute case to the open receivable item.
  • The status Not Justified must be saved correctly.
  • The collection worklist generation job must run successfully.
  • You must assign the collector correctly, otherwise the worklist may not appear for the user.

Also, in BFC, the worklist is not always immediately created after changing the dispute status. The BFC process flow shows that collections worklists are created through the scheduled/nightly worklist creation run.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://media-01.imu.nl/storage/bestpractices.scheer-nederland.com/9172/bfc-2111-s4hana-cloud-process-flow.pdf?

I suggest you carry out the following checks in your system:

  1. Open Manage Dispute Cases. Confirm the dispute case status is 23. Check that the dispute case is linked to the correct customer open item.                                                                                                 
  2. Open Schedule Accounts Receivable Jobs, check whether the collection worklist jobs have run successfully.                        
  3. Open Process Collection Worklist. Search the correct collection specialist, company code, customer, and due date range. If nothing appears, check the customer’s collection profile and collection segment assignment.                        
  4. If all settings are correct and the item still does not appear, raise a case with SAP and reference KBA 3711867.

 

To summarise:

Changing a dispute case to Not Justified does not by itself guarantee immediate visibility in Process Collection Worklist. The customer and open item must be relevant for collections, the dispute must be linked to the receivable, and the collection worklist generation job must run successfully. First check the collection profile, collection segment, collector assignment, and job log. Rais a case if worklist still does not appear.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Your S/4HANA & Ariba implementation is live, but your GRC ruleset do not have Ariba rules configured
entry.content:
Most SAP GRC implementations have a blind spot.

It is called Ariba.

And if your SoD ruleset doesn't account for it, you may be signing off clean access reviews on a system that's wide open to procurement fraud.

Across numerous SAP GRC implementations and Pre Go-Live and Post Go-Live reviews across the region, I typically get one of three responses when I ask whether the SoD ruleset covers cross-application risks between S/4HANA and Ariba:

  • "SAP GRC doesn't support integration with Ariba."
  • "We don't have IAG (Identity Access Governance)."
  • "We are planning to do it as part of Phase 2."

Let me translate those for you:

  • means "we haven't figured it out yet"
  • means "we know it's a gap but haven't prioritised it yet"
  • means "we are hoping the auditors don't notice before Phase 2"

That’s not a configuration gap. That’s your next audit finding !!!

The Integration Gap

1. GRC has no native Ariba connector.

Unlike the standard S/4HANA connector that pulls user assignments, role assignments, and authorisation objects, Ariba is opaque to GRC. There's no out-of-the-box integration. 

2. Ariba doesn't expose authorisations the way S/4HANA does.

There is no PFCG equivalent where you can drill into what a user can actually do in Ariba. No authorisation objects. No field-level permissions. Just system groups with pre-defined permissions.

3. In practice, this means a user could be registering fictitious suppliers in Ariba and processing invoices in S/4HANA and your GRC system would see nothing wrong.

So What Can You Do?

Here's the practical workaround I have implemented across multiple clients. The workaround is elegant in its simplicity where we bring Ariba into GRC's language by representing Ariba groups as custom Tcodes and template roles in S/4HANA.

To gain a better understanding of the SAP Ariba authorisation design, please review the list of access groups available in Ariba using the link below.

https://help.sap.com/docs/strategic-sourcing/strategic-sourcing-and-supplier-management-group-descriptions/strategic-sourcing-and-supplier-management-group-descriptions

SAP Ariba uses a group-based authorisation model where access is determined entirely by group membership.

Standard groups delivered in Ariba have fixed, pre-defined permissions that cannot be modified. While customers can create custom groups, these are essentially collections of standard groups (through group nesting) and do not allow individual permissions or actions to be customised, unlike PFCG roles.

Unlike PFCG roles, you cannot hand-pick permissions across Ariba groups. The permission boundary is always the standard groups and custom groups are simply collections of those standard groups.

For cross-application SoD analysis, the assessment should be performed between the user's assigned Ariba groups and their S/4HANA roles/access. As Ariba access is governed by group membership, the effective permissions granted by the Ariba groups should be evaluated together with the user's S/4HANA access to identify potential SoD conflicts.

Example

This is a standard SAP Ariba system group. If I want to create a custom group called "Supplier Onboarding Team Member (SUPP_ONB_TEAM_MEM)", I would create the custom group and assign the relevant system group as its parent group. The custom group would then inherit the permissions from that system group.

The Solution

Step 1: Create Custom Tcodes for Ariba System Groups. A sample is shown in the below screenshot. The Tcode descriptions can be written in simple, business-friendly language so that end users can easily understand the purpose of each group without requiring technical knowledge.

Step 2: Create template or dummy roles for ARIBA groups in S4HANA. Let us assume that you have an ARIBA group with name "Supplier Onboarding Team Member", you can create a PFCG role as per your own naming convention.

ARIBA GroupS/4HANA role nameDescription
Supplier Onboarding Team MemberAR:S_SUPPLIER_ONBOARDING_TEAMSee screenshot below

Maintain a comprehensive description of the role, including all actions that can be performed by users assigned to it in Ariba. This enables more effective access reviews, role design validation, and SoD risk assessments.

With the above approach, SAP Ariba groups are represented as template (dummy) roles in SAP S/4HANA, while Ariba system groups are represented as custom Tcodes in SAP S/4HANA.

Step 3:  This is the critical step. Without it, Steps 1 and 2 are just housekeeping. Mapping the Ariba Tcodes into your GRC ruleset is what transforms a workaround into a functioning control.  This will enable access risk analysis to identify potential Segregation of Duties (SoD) conflicts and critical access risks arising from Ariba groups. The custom Ariba Tcodes should be mapped to existing business functions and risk definitions within the Ruleset. New functions and risks may also be created to address Ariba-specific activities.

This approach ensures that Ariba access assignments, whether provisioned directly or through composite roles, are included in access risk analysis and remain subject to the client's governance, risk, and compliance controls.

Here is a real SoD risk that spans both systems and would be completely missed without this approach. Maintain a fictitious vendor and enter a Vendor invoice for automatic payment

  • Vendor Master Maintenance (ZPR01) + Process Vendor Invoices (ZAP02)

I have included the ARIBA Tcodes related to the supplier maintenance in this Vendor Master Maintenance function.

When a user who has access to process vendor invoices is being granted with the Supplier Onboarding Team Member (Ref S4HANA Role: AR:S_SUPPLIER_ONBOARDING_TEAM) group in ARIBA, the corresponding SOD risks will be highlighted as shown in the below screenshot.

The SoD risk didn't exist in S/4HANA alone. It didn't exist in Ariba alone. It existed in the combination of access across both systems and was  invisible to  GRC ruleset, invisible to most access reviews, and invisible to auditors who don't know to look for it.

That's the gap this approach closes.

Your GRC ruleset should reflect the reality of how your users actually work across applications and not within silos. If Ariba is part of your procurement landscape and those functions are not in your ruleset, your SoD analysis is not complete.

Don't let your blind spot become your audit finding.

Extending the GRC Ruleset — What Else Can You Define?

The supplier onboarding example above is just one of many cross-system SoD conflicts that become visible once Ariba is brought into your GRC ruleset.

Using the same custom Tcodes approach, you can define risks across the full procurement lifecycle. Here are some examples:

ARIBA AccessS/4HANA AccessPotential Risk Scenario
Register / onboard suppliersExecute payment runFictitious vendor fraud
Electronically sign contractsProcess vendor invoicesContract approval + Invoice processing by same person
Create sourcing projects & requisitionsPost goods receiptsFull P2P cycle without segregation
Mass update contractsMaintain purchase ordersBulk reprice contracts then self-raise POs

The pattern is consistent i.e., the fraud doesn't live in one system. It lives in the combination.

Once your Ariba groups are represented as custom Tcodes in GRC, any of these risks can be configured, monitored and reported just like any native S/4HANA SoD conflict.

Thank you for reading. I hope this gives you a practical starting point for bridging the Ariba–S/4HANA SoD coverage gap in your GRC implementation.

If you have questions, have implemented something similar, or want to share your own approach, please drop a comment below.

You can also reach me directly at Maddysmail26@gmail.com whether it's a quick question, a deeper implementation challenge, or just a conversation about SAP.

Let's continue learning, sharing and building on each other's experience 🙂

entry.title: Alternative Standard Mobile Application for Batch-Managed Outbound Deliveries Without EWM
entry.content:
Hello,

In our customer's system, warehouse operators are expected to perform goods issue processes using handheld barcode scanning devices in production sites where SAP EWM will not be implemented.

We evaluated the Pick Outbound Delivery application for this purpose. However, when an outbound delivery contains batch-managed materials, the application displays the following error message:

LS_SHP022
"Delivery contains items with batches and cannot be maintained."

Therefore, we are unable to recommend this application for our scenario.

We would appreciate your guidance on the following questions:

Is there any configuration, activation, or recommended approach that would allow the Pick Outbound Delivery application to be used with batch-managed materials?
If this is a standard product limitation, which standard SAP application is recommended in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud without EWM for warehouse operators using handheld devices to perform the following activities?

  • Select or scan an outbound delivery
  • Select or confirm the batch
  • Confirm the picked quantity
  • Post Goods Issue (PGI)

entry.title: Unable to Configure Activities for "Add Development Goal" Event in SAP SuccessFactors ISC
entry.content:
Product Area:
SAP SuccessFactors Intelligent Services Center (ISC)

Hi Experts,

We are currently working on a business requirement in SAP SuccessFactors where managers should receive a notification/email whenever an employee adds a development goal.

While exploring SAP SuccessFactors Intelligent Services Center (ISC), we identified the "Add Development Goal" event as a potential trigger. However, when we access this event in ISC, the following message is displayed:

"There are no activities enabled for this flow. Add activities from the activity library."

The issue is that we are unable to create or add any activities from the Activity Library for this flow. As a result, we cannot configure notifications or email actions related to the "Add Development Goal" event.

Our Objective:

  • Trigger a notification or email to the employee's manager whenever a development goal is added.
  • Use ISC event-based processing rather than custom solutions, if possible.

What We Have Observed:

  • The "Add Development Goal" flow is visible in ISC.
  • No activities are currently available for the flow.
  • We do not see an option to create or enable activities for this event.
  • Therefore, notifications cannot be configured for this scenario.

Questions:

  1. Is the "Add Development Goal" ISC event intended to support activities and notifications?
  2. Are there any prerequisites or configuration steps required to enable activities for this flow?
  3. Has anyone successfully configured manager notifications when employees add development goals?
  4. Is there an alternative recommended approach (Business Rules, Workflow, Alert & Notification Framework, etc.) to achieve this requirement?

Any guidance, documentation references, or implementation experiences would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: Re: Plant language change from EN to JA ( Japanese) for JP00
entry.content:
Hi Manoj_Gheewala,

  1. Go to the SAP Easy Access screen.
  2. Navigate to System → User Profile → Own Data.
  3. Open the Defaults tab (or Address tab depending on the SAP release/version).
  4. In the Logon Language field, change the language from EN to JA.
  5. Save the changes and log on again for the language change to take effect.

Or using transaction code:

  1. Execute transaction SU3.
  2. In the Defaults tab, change Logon Language from EN to JA, save the changes, and log on again.

Best regards,

Thu Mai

entry.title: Re: SAP Integration Suite - Connecting to S/4HANA Private Cloud OData with OAuth (Client Credentials
entry.content:
Hi @JaiSreenivasan ,

thank you for your question. The list below provides an overview of the authentication and SSO mechanisms that you can use for authentication at HTTP transport level for providing and consuming Web services on SAP Netweaver Application Server:

  • Standard Authentication (User ID and Password)
  • X.509 Certificates
  • Logon Tickets

As you can see OAuth is not supported for web services. 

Further details are available through this link. 

BR

Frederick

entry.title: How to Automatically Assign Default Business Roles to New Users in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and manage users through the Maintain Business Users application.

We have a requirement to automatically assign a set of default business roles whenever a new business user is created. Currently, the role assignment is performed manually, which becomes time-consuming and prone to errors when onboarding a large number of users.

We would like to understand:

  • Is there any standard API available to assign business roles to users in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud?
  • Is there a way to automate business role assignment during or after user creation?
  • Are there any recommended approaches using SAP Cloud Identity Services, SAP Integration Suite, SAP BTP, or other SAP-supported integration options?
  • Has anyone implemented a similar solution in a Public Cloud environment?

Our objective is to ensure that newly created users automatically receive a predefined set of business roles without requiring manual intervention.

Any guidance, best practices, or references to relevant SAP documentation would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,
Siva Nagi Reddy

entry.title: How to Prevent Duplicate GST Numbers in Customer and Supplier Master Data in SAP S/4HANA Public Clou
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We are using SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud and have a business requirement related to GST number validation in Business Partner master data.

The business wants to ensure that the GST Number (Tax Number) maintained in a Customer or Supplier master record is unique across the system.

The expected behavior is as follows:

  • When a user creates or updates a Customer or Supplier master record and enters a GST number, the system should automatically check whether the same GST number already exists in any existing Customer or Supplier master record.
  • If the GST number does not exist, the system should allow the record to be saved.
  • If the GST number already exists, the system should prevent the save operation and display an appropriate error message indicating that a duplicate GST number exists.

We are aware of the Configure Matching and Duplicate Check (SSCUI 100274) functionality. However, our requirement is to enforce a hard validation during save, rather than only identifying potential duplicates.

Could you please advise:

  • Is there any standard functionality available in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to enforce uniqueness of GST numbers across Business Partners?
  • Can this requirement be achieved through SSCUI configuration, Business Partner duplicate checks, validation rules, or key user extensibility?
  • Has anyone implemented a similar requirement in a Public Cloud environment?
  • Are there any SAP-recommended best practices for preventing duplicate GST registrations?

Any guidance, documentation references, or implementation experiences would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,
Siva Nagi Reddy

entry.title: Why You Can’t Edit or Reply to a case (and How to Fix It)
entry.content:
Why You Can’t Edit or Reply to a case (and How to Fix It) 

 

 

 To report an SAP support case, you must have a valid S-user ID with the required authorizations. This includes authorization to create support cases and customer must have a valid maintenance for the product involved; otherwise, case creation may not be available. 

Additional case-related authorizations may also be required, as outlined below. 

 Display Cases  

Display all Cases.  

Report a Technical Issue  

Send Cases to SAP  

Close Cases  

Display Cloud Data (only needed for some Cloud Solutions KBA 2598170 - What is the Display Cloud Data authorization? - SAP for Me - SAP for Me 

 

 

One can report SAP case via https://me.sap.com/getsupport  and there are scenarios as below when you are unable to edit/reply to the case. 

 

  • Cannot edit a case 
  • Cannot reply to a case 
  • Cannot see a case 

 You can come across a situation, that you are unable to edit/reply to the case.  

Example like, when a case has the status of ‘Confirmed’ or ‘Confirmed Automatically’, that means it is closed and cannot be edited or reopened.  You may refer the SAP KBA note 2537073 - Can a closed (Confirmed/Confirmed Automatically) case be reopened? - SAP for Me - SAP for Me  for detailed information. 

 

 

Please note: That any cases that are Confirmed / Closed by the customer can no longer be reopened.  

Confirmed Automatically cases or confirmed automatically by the system means the case was in the customer’s queue for 21 days and case was not sent to SAP queue. 

 Kindly refer the detailed SAP KBA note#1929454 - What is the time for automatic closure of support cases? - SAP for Me - SAP for Me 

 

In that situation a new case needs to be reported related to a previous case that is now closed you can refer to the closed case in the problem description if needed. 

 

 You might encounter a scenario where you can access existing cases in SAP but are unable to submit new ones due to insufficient user authorizations. 

 

For instance, you might see an error message like: "Error: You are not authorized to submit cases for installation 12xxx7. Please contact your administrator for assistance." 

  In this situation, you can use the self-service authorization request to request the authorization - see KBA 2891528 and you can also refer to  Authorization Concept for detailed information. 
 
S-user ID that lack a particular authorization can request it through the Self-Service Authorization Request feature.   

  • Go to My Authorizations and Functions (or click the Profile icon top right of SAP for Me, click My Settings, click Authorizations and Functions tab) 
  • Click the AVAILABLE AUTHORIZATIONS tab to see existing S-user authorizations  
  • Click Request Authorization (top right) 
     

  •  

    Complete the Authorization Request form 
  • Click Submit 
     

  •  

    The request will be displayed in the REQUESTS tab with Awaiting approval status 
     

  Thanks for reading!  

We hope you found this helpful  
  
We would love to hear from you, let us know what you think?  

  
Read more Customer Interaction Center blog posts Here  

 If you have any questions, please feel free to ask us in the Q&A All Questions in Customer Experience | SAP Community and All Questions in Support Services | SAP Community   
 
You can also explore the Customer Enablement Program details through CIC Webcasts, where you’ll find information on previously recorded sessions, upcoming webcasts, SAP Community Blogs, and videos covering a variety of topics., where you’ll find information on previously recorded sessions, upcoming webcasts, SAP Community Blogs, and videos covering a variety of topics.

entry.title: Building an LLM Agent in CAP Java with Spring AI
entry.content:
Agentic systems are all the rage these days. Previously I showed how to implement an LLM agent in CAP using the CAP Node.js runtime. Let's look at the CAP Java side.

Agenda:

  • Overview
  • Initialize the CAP app
  • Connecting to SAP AI Core with the SAP AI SDK
  • Spring AI: Calling an agent
  • Spring AI: Calling a tool
  • CAP: Create a data model and a service
  • Calling the agent in CAP with a query tool (read)
  • Calling the agent in CAP with an action tool (write)
  • What's next?

Overview

First, what is an agent? There are many definitions out there, but here are a few that I like:

"Agents combine language models with tools to create systems that can reason about tasks, decide which tools to use, and iteratively work towards solutions." - langchain
"Agents are systems that independently accomplish tasks on your behalf." - openai
"An LLM in a loop with an objective" - Simon Willison
"An LLM, some tools and a loop meet in a bar" - me

That last one makes for some good jokes.

In essence, the agent orchestrates the interaction between the model, the tools, and the user.

The tool output is fed back into the model as context for the next step, allowing the agent to iteratively work towards a solution. The loop is continued until the model outputs a final answer or meets a predefined stopping condition like a maximum iteration count. This is what allows agents to handle complex tasks that require multiple steps and interactions with external systems.

TLDR

If you just want a quick example, here you go. If you want to understand how to integrate agents into a CAP Java application step by step, read on.

// .../agent/TravelAgent.java
@Configuration
public class TravelAgent {
  @Autowired TravelTools tools;

  @Bean
  ChatClient agent() {
    OrchestrationModuleConfig config = new OrchestrationModuleConfig().withLlmConfig(OrchestrationAiModel.MISTRAL_SMALL);
    OrchestrationChatOptions opts = new OrchestrationChatOptions(config);
    ChatModel model = new OrchestrationChatModel();
    return ChatClient.builder(model)
      .defaultOptions(opts)
      .defaultSystem("Today is " + LocalDate.now())
      .defaultTools(tools)
      .build();
  }
}
// .../agent/TravelTools.java
@Component
public class TravelTools {
  @Autowired TravelService travelService;
  @Autowired CdsRuntime runtime;

  @Tool
  List<Trips> searchTrips(
    @ToolParam(description = "begin date") LocalDate beginDate,
    @ToolParam(description = "end date") LocalDate endDate
  ) {
    var query = Select.from(Trips_.class)
      .where(t -> t.beginDate().ge(beginDate) .and( t.endDate().le(endDate) ));
    var result = travelService.run(query);
    return result.list();
  }
}
// .../handler/TravelHandler.java
@Component @ServiceName(TravelService_.CDS_NAME)
public class TravelHandler implements EventHandler {
  @Autowired ChatClient agent;

  @On
  String invokeAgent(InvokeAgentContext context) {
    return agent.prompt(context.getInput()).call().content();
  }
}

On libraries and workflows

In the Java world, there are multiple libraries to choose from, for example langchain4j and Spring AI. Since both CAP Java and the SAP AI SDK already integrate well with Spring and Spring AI, we use Spring AI in this post.

Searching for building agents with Spring AI, we most prominently find a blog post on building effective agents from January 2025. It is based on a similarly named post by Anthropic, which describes the important distinction between workflows and agents. Workflows are predefined sequences of steps, while agents are running in a feedback loop with their environment until they reach a stopping point.

The Spring AI documentation mainly focuses on manual workflows, which can be misleading when it comes to building an agent. The Spring AI ChatClient already includes the core agentic loop. This is also highlighted further in newer blog posts about Spring AI, for example with exposing generic agent skills as a tool and a2a integration.

Initialize the CAP app

To get started, we will initialize a new CAP project using the cds init command. This will create a new directory with the necessary files and folders for our CAP application. For this tutorial, we will use the Java runtime. If you are interested in CAP Node.js instead, check this blog post.

cds init cap-agent --java
code cap-agent  # open in vscode

Connecting to SAP AI Core with the SAP AI SDK

We will use the SAP AI SDK to connect our CAP application to the SAP AI Core service. This will allow us to call a variety of language models from our CAP application.

First, make sure that you have an SAP AI Core service instance. The setup is described here: https://help.sap.com/docs/sap-ai-core/sap-ai-core-service-guide/initial-setup

If using the Cloud Foundry cli, you can create a service instance like this:

cf create-service aicore extended agent-ai

Now we can use cds bind to create a service key and save a reference to it in .cdsrc-private.json. This will come in handy later when we want to try it out.

cds bind -2 agent-ai

Next add the SAP AI SDK and Spring AI dependencies:

<!-- srv/pom.xml -->
<dependencies>
  ...
  <dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.ai</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-ai-commons</artifactId>
  </dependency>
  <dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.ai</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-ai-model</artifactId>
  </dependency>
  <dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.ai</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-ai-client-chat</artifactId>
  </dependency>
  <dependency>
    <groupId>com.sap.ai.sdk</groupId>
    <artifactId>orchestration</artifactId>
    <version>${ai-sdk.version}</version>
  </dependency>
</dependencies>
<!-- pom.xml -->
<properties>
  ...
  <!-- Use the newest versions here -->
  <spring-ai.version>1.1.7</spring-ai.version>
  <ai-sdk.version>1.19.0</ai-sdk.version>
</properties>
<dependencyManagement>
  <dependencies>
    ...
    <dependency>
      <groupId>org.springframework.ai</groupId>
      <artifactId>spring-ai-bom</artifactId>
      <version>${spring-ai.version}</version>
      <type>pom</type>
      <scope>import</scope>
    </dependency>
  </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>

We can test out our connection by creating a simple test:

// src/test/java/customer/cap_agent/AgentTest.java
package customer.cap_agent;

import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertTrue;

import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;
import org.springframework.ai.chat.model.ChatModel;
import org.springframework.ai.chat.model.ChatResponse;
import org.springframework.ai.chat.prompt.Prompt;

import com.sap.ai.sdk.orchestration.OrchestrationAiModel;
import com.sap.ai.sdk.orchestration.OrchestrationModuleConfig;
import com.sap.ai.sdk.orchestration.spring.OrchestrationChatModel;
import com.sap.ai.sdk.orchestration.spring.OrchestrationChatOptions;

public class AgentTest {
  private final static Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(AgentTest.class);

  private final OrchestrationChatOptions opts = new OrchestrationChatOptions(
    new OrchestrationModuleConfig()
      .withLlmConfig(OrchestrationAiModel.MISTRAL_SMALL));

  @ Test
  void testSdkPrompt() {
    ChatModel model = new OrchestrationChatModel();
    Prompt prompt = new Prompt("What is the capital of France?", opts);
    ChatResponse response = model.call(prompt);
    String content = response.getResult().getOutput().getText();
    log.info(content);
    assertTrue(content.contains("Paris"));
  }
}

Now let's utilize our earlier cds bind reference to run the test with the correct environment variables:

cds bind --exec -- mvn test -Dtest=customer.cap_agent.AgentTest#testSdkPrompt

The cds bind --exec command will set the environment variables from the service key we created earlier, so that the SAP AI SDK can authenticate to the SAP AI Core service.

The nice thing about cds bind --exec is that it retrieves the credentials ad-hoc, so they are not saved on disk. However, we cannot dynamically inject environment variables into the Java test runner in VSCode. So if we want to run the tests from VSCode, we need to save the environment variables in a file first. We can do this with the following command:

cds bind --exec -- node -e "require('fs').writeFileSync('.hybrid.env', 'VCAP_SERVICES=' + process.env.VCAP_SERVICES, 'utf8')"

Make sure that the file is in the .gitignore:

# .gitignore
*.env

Then we configure the test runner to use this .hybrid.env file:

// .vscode/settings.json
"java.test.config": [
   {
     "name": "cap-bound-tests",
     "workingDirectory": "${workspaceFolder}",
     "envFile": "${workspaceFolder}/.hybrid.env"
   }
 ]

Spring AI: Calling an agent

If you're familiar with agents, you can skip this section and jump directly to calling an agent from CAP. But if you're new to agents, this section is for you.

As mentioned earlier, agents combine language models with tools. Leaving out the tools, an agent behaves similarly to a direct model call. Let's add a simple test to verify this:

// src/test/java/customer/cap_agent/AgentTest.java
  @ Test
  void testPrompt() {
    ChatModel model = new OrchestrationChatModel();
    ChatClient agent = ChatClient.builder(model).defaultOptions(opts).build();
    String content = agent.prompt("What is the capital of France?").call().content();
    log.info(content);
    assertTrue(content.contains("Paris"));
  }
cds bind --exec -- mvn test -Dtest=customer.cap_agent.AgentTest#testPrompt

Now this is a very simple agent that doesn't do much more than the model itself, but it sets the stage for adding tools and iterating in a loop based on tool output, which is where the real power of agents comes in.

Spring AI: Calling a tool

Let's say we have some data about available trips...

record Trip (LocalDate beginDate, LocalDate endDate, String description) {};

List<Trip> availableTrips = List.of(
  new Trip(
    LocalDate.parse("2026-06-13"),
    LocalDate.parse("2026-06-14"),
    "Weekend trip to Heidelberg featuring castle sightseeing, hiking and excellent cuisine."),
  new Trip(
    LocalDate.parse("2026-06-13"),
    LocalDate.parse("2026-06-14"),
    "Daytrip to Europa-Park with exciting rollercoasters.")
);

We can now define a method to search for trips based on a time frame.

public List<Trip> searchTrips(LocalDate beginDate, LocalDate endDate) {
  return availableTrips.stream().filter(
    t ->  !t.beginDate.isBefore(beginDate) &&
          !t.endDate.isAfter(endDate)).toList();
}

Spring AI allows us to expose this method as a @Tool that the agent can call.

@Tool(description = "Search trips by time frame")
List<Trip> searchTrips(
  @ToolParam(description = "Begin date") LocalDate beginDate,
  @ToolParam(description = "End date") LocalDate endDate
) {
  log.info("[tool call]: searchTrips " + beginDate.toString() + " - " + endDate.toString());
  return availableTrips.stream().filter(
    t ->  !t.beginDate.isBefore(beginDate) &&
          !t.endDate.isAfter(endDate)).toList();
}

Since Java is already a typed language, the method signature can be used by Spring AI to automatically generate a JSON schema for the tool. The provided descriptions are also used in the schema, allowing the model to understand when and how to use the tool.

⚠️Important Note: The schema only advises the model and JSON parsing only validates structural correctness. It is still possible for the agent to provide semantically incorrect inputs that pass validation. This can also be maliciously exploited, so the inputs need to be handled as untrusted data. For example, be aware of potential SQL injection if the tool interacts with a database.

Now we can add the tool to our agent and test it out:

// src/test/java/customer/cap_agent/AgentTest.java
  class TravelTools {
    record Trip (LocalDate beginDate, LocalDate endDate, String description) {};

    List<Trip> availableTrips = List.of(
      new Trip(
        LocalDate.parse("2026-06-13"),
        LocalDate.parse("2026-06-14"),
        "Weekend trip to Heidelberg featuring castle sightseeing, hiking and excellent cuisine."),
      new Trip(
        LocalDate.parse("2026-06-13"),
        LocalDate.parse("2026-06-14"),
        "Daytrip to Europa-Park with exciting rollercoasters.")
    );

    @Tool(description = "Search trips by time frame")
    List<Trip> searchTrips(
      @ToolParam(description = "Begin date") LocalDate beginDate,
      @ToolParam(description = "End date") LocalDate endDate
    ) {
      log.info("[tool call]: searchTrips " + beginDate.toString() + " - " + endDate.toString());
      return availableTrips.stream().filter(
        t ->  !t.beginDate.isBefore(beginDate) &&
              !t.endDate.isAfter(endDate)).toList();
    }
  }

  @ Test
  void testTool() {
    ChatModel model = new OrchestrationChatModel();
    ChatClient agent = ChatClient.builder(model)
      .defaultOptions(opts)
      .defaultSystem("Today is 2026-06-10") // hardcoded date to make the test deterministic
      .defaultTools(new TravelTools())
      .build();
    String content = agent.prompt("Is there a trip next weekend?").call().content();
    log.info(content);
    assertTrue(content.contains("Heidelberg"));
  }
cds bind --exec -- mvn test -Dtest=customer.cap_agent.AgentTest#testTool

The agent should recognize that the user is asking for a trip recommendation, decide to use the searchTrips tool and use the answer for the final response.

Notice that we've also adjusted the system prompt to include the "current" date:

      .defaultSystem("Today is 2026-06-10") // hardcoded date to make the test deterministic

If we do not provide the current date, the model typically falls back to its training data... in my case, July 2024. Not quite ideal for planning a weekend trip in 2026. Feel free to experiment with different dates and see how it affects the agent's behavior. You can also try asking for trips on specific dates or with different criteria to see how the agent utilizes the tool.

CAP: Create a data model and a service

Now that we know how to build an agent, let's see how we can integrate it into a CAP application with a real data model and service.

First, let's create a simple data model for storing trip information. We can define this in the db/schema.cds file:

// db/schema.cds
namespace example.travel;
using { User } from '@sap/cds/common';


entity Trips {
  key ID       : Integer @readonly;
  description  : String(2048);
  beginDate    : Date default $now;
  endDate      : Date default $now;
  bookingFee   : Decimal(9,4) default 0;
  currency     : String(3) default 'EUR';
  agency       : Association to Agencies;
  bookings     : Association to many Bookings on bookings.trip = $self;
}

entity Agencies {
    key ID : Integer @readonly;
    name   : String(256);
}


entity Bookings {
  key trip        : Association to Trips;
  key user        : User @readonly @(cds.on.insert: $user);
      bookingDate : Date default $now;
}

Then define a customer facing service in srv/travel-service.cds:

// srv/travel-service.cds
using { example.travel } from '../db/schema';

@odata
service TravelService {
    @readonly entity Trips as projection on travel.Trips
    actions {
        action book() returns String;
    };

    @readonly entity Agencies as projection on travel.Agencies;
    @readonly entity Bookings as projection on travel.Bookings where $user = user;
}

This service exposes the Trips and Agencies entities and also defines a custom action book for booking a trip. We will implement the logic for this action later.

Next, we need some sample data. Always use cds add data to add syntactically correct sample data. Let's add some trips and agencies:

cds add data -n 5 --filter Agencies
cds add data -n 10 --filter Trips

You can use an LLM to generate more meaningful test data while adhering to the existing schema. For example:

db/data/example.travel-Trips.csv

ID,description,beginDate,endDate,bookingFee,currency,agency_ID
2001,"7-Night Maldives Overwater Bungalow Retreat — private chef dinners, unlimited snorkeling excursions, sunset dolphin cruise, and couples spa all included",2026-06-14,2026-06-21,4299.0000,EUR,1001
2002,"14-Night Grand Mediterranean Yacht Odyssey — sail Barcelona to Athens with stops in Ibiza, Amalfi, and Santorini; premium open bar, fine dining, and water sports throughout",2026-07-05,2026-07-19,6850.0000,EUR,1002
2003,"10-Night Bali Spiritual & Adventure Escape — morning yoga, volcano trekking, rice terrace cycling, nightly Kecak fire dance performances, and full board at a jungle resort",2026-08-01,2026-08-11,3199.0000,USD,1003
2004,"5-Night Dubai Ultra-Luxury City Break — penthouse at Burj Al Arab, desert falcon safari, helicopter city tour, and Michelin-starred dinners every evening",2026-09-10,2026-09-15,7499.0000,USD,1004
2005,"12-Night African Safari & Zanzibar Beach Combo — Great Migration in the Serengeti, game drives at dusk, private Zanzibar beach resort, spice farm tour, all meals included",2026-10-02,2026-10-14,5975.0000,EUR,1005
2006,"8-Night Norway Northern Lights Adventure — dog sledding, ice hotel overnight stay, snowmobile aurora tour, reindeer farm visit, and traditional Viking feast",2027-01-15,2027-01-23,5250.0000,EUR,1006
2007,"11-Night Thailand Island Hopper — Koh Samui, Koh Phi Phi, and Phuket; speedboat transfers, beach parties, Muay Thai show, and a gourmet Thai cooking class",2026-11-08,2026-11-19,2899.0000,USD,1007
2008,"9-Night Amalfi Coast & Tuscany Gourmet Tour — private villa stays, truffle hunting, wine tasting at five vineyards, pasta-making masterclass, and a private boat day",2026-05-20,2026-05-29,5100.0000,EUR,1008
2009,"6-Night Cancún All-Inclusive Sun & Surf — beachfront resort, unlimited cocktails, catamaran party boat, cenote snorkeling, and a Mayan ruins guided day trip",2026-12-01,2026-12-07,1999.0000,USD,1009
2010,"15-Night New Zealand Adventure Circuit — bungee jumping in Queenstown, Milford Sound cruise, hobbit village tour, Rotorua geothermal spa, and Tongariro Alpine Crossing",2027-03-10,2027-03-25,7200.0000,EUR,1010

db/data/example.travel-Agencies.csv

ID,name
1001,SunEscape Holidays
1002,Wanderlust All-Inclusive
1003,Tropical Getaways Ltd.
1004,Azure Horizon Travel
1005,Paradise Found Agency
1006,Elite Escapes International
1007,Oceanic Dreams Travel
1008,Grand Voyage Co.
1009,Blissful Journeys
1010,The Luxury Travel Bureau

 

Run the application with

mvn cds:watch

You can now navigate to http://localhost:8080/ and inspect the api endpoints and the generated test data.

Calling the agent in CAP with a query tool (read)

First, let's save our agent into its own Spring Bean so that we can inject it into our service implementation:

// src/main/java/customer/cap_agent/agent/TravelAgent.java
package customer.cap_agent.agent;

import java.time.LocalDate;

import org.springframework.ai.chat.client.ChatClient;
import org.springframework.ai.chat.model.ChatModel;
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean;
import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration;

import com.sap.ai.sdk.orchestration.OrchestrationAiModel;
import com.sap.ai.sdk.orchestration.OrchestrationModuleConfig;
import com.sap.ai.sdk.orchestration.spring.OrchestrationChatModel;
import com.sap.ai.sdk.orchestration.spring.OrchestrationChatOptions;

@Configuration
public class TravelAgent {

  @Autowired
  TravelTools tools;

  @Bean
  ChatClient agent() {
    OrchestrationModuleConfig config = new OrchestrationModuleConfig().withLlmConfig(OrchestrationAiModel.MISTRAL_SMALL);
    OrchestrationChatOptions opts = new OrchestrationChatOptions(config);
    ChatModel model = new OrchestrationChatModel();
    return ChatClient.builder(model)
      .defaultOptions(opts)
      .defaultSystem("Today is " + LocalDate.now())
      .defaultTools(tools)
      .build();
  }
}

To the fun part: integrating the agent into our CAP application. We will create a new tool that allows the agent to query available trips based on a date range, similar to the one we created in the testing section, but this time it will query our actual data model.

// src/main/java/customer/cap_agent/agent/TravelTools.java
package customer.cap_agent.agent;

import java.time.LocalDate;
import java.util.List;
import org.slf4j.Logger;
import org.slf4j.LoggerFactory;
import org.springframework.ai.tool.annotation.Tool;
import org.springframework.ai.tool.annotation.ToolParam;
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

import com.sap.cds.ql.Select;

import cds.gen.travelservice.TravelService;
import cds.gen.travelservice.Trips;
import cds.gen.travelservice.Trips_;

@Component
public class TravelTools {
  private final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(TravelTools.class);

  @Autowired
  TravelService travelService;

  @Tool
  List<Trips> searchTrips(
    @ToolParam(description = "begin date") LocalDate beginDate,
    @ToolParam(description = "end date") LocalDate endDate
  ) {
    log.info("[tool call]: searchTrips " + beginDate + " - " + endDate);
    var query = Select.from(Trips_.class)
      .where(t -> t.beginDate().ge(beginDate) .and( t.endDate().le(endDate) ));
    var result = travelService.run(query);
    return result.list();
  }
}

Inspecting the tool implementation, we use a Java CQL Select with a where clause checking the date range.

var query = Select.from(Trips_.class)
  .where(t -> t.beginDate().ge(beginDate) .and( t.endDate().le(endDate) ));

Note that the beginDate and endDate tool inputs are automatically converted to query parameters:

SELECT ... WHERE T0."BEGINDATE" >= ? and T0."ENDDATE" <= ?

This is normal in CAP Java, and it is important, as the agent might provide malicious inputs that could lead to SQL injection if not handled properly. You can also check the SQL statements yourself by setting the log level for com.sap.cds.persistence.sql to DEBUG:

# srv/src/main/resources/application.yaml
logging.level:
  com.sap.cds.persistence.sql: DEBUG

We can then execute the query using the injected TravelService, which will ensure that all the usual service logic is applied, including user permissions.

var result = travelService.run(query);

Since we call the travel service, we use cds.gen.travelservice.Trips_ and cds.gen.travelservice.Trips as model and accessor interfaces.

Next, let's call the agent from our service implementation. In srv/travel-service.cds, we define the invokeAgent action, allowing us to call the agent over HTTP.

// srv/travel-service.cds
@odata
service TravelService {
    ...
    action invokeAgent(input: String) returns String;
}

If you still have mvn cds:watch running, the cds model should be recompiled automatically. If not, run mvn compile to compile the model and generate the necessary Java classes for the new action.

Then we add the handler implementation which simply calls the agent.

// src/main/java/customer/cap_agent/handler/TravelHandler.java
package customer.cap_agent.handler;

import org.springframework.ai.chat.client.ChatClient;
import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

import com.sap.cds.services.handler.EventHandler;
import com.sap.cds.services.handler.annotations.On;
import com.sap.cds.services.handler.annotations.ServiceName;

import cds.gen.travelservice.TravelService_;
import cds.gen.travelservice.InvokeAgentContext;

@Component
@ServiceName(TravelService_.CDS_NAME)
public class TravelHandler implements EventHandler {
  @Autowired
  ChatClient agent;

  @On
  String invokeAgent(InvokeAgentContext context) {
    return agent.prompt(context.getInput()).call().content();
  }
}

Run the application with

cds bind --exec -- mvn cds:watch

This utilizes our earlier cds bind command, so we can call the agent with the correct credentials for SAP AI Core.

To invoke the agent, create a test/http/TravelService.http file using cds add http. Since we chose the @odataprotocol, we can call our action at /odata/v4/TravelService/invokeAgent:

# test/http/TravelService.http
@server=http://localhost:8080
@username=authenticated
@password=

### invokeAgent
# @NAME invokeAgent_POST
POST {{server}}/odata/v4/TravelService/invokeAgent
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Basic {{username}}:{{password}}

{
  "input": "What are exciting trips for this summer? I like to go to the beach"
}

Since we added logging to our tool, you should observe the agent calling the searchTrips tool with the appropriate date range for summer:

[tool call]: searchTrips 2026-06-01 - 2026-08-31

The response contains the final answer from the agent, which should include the available trips for this summer.

This shows the agentic ReAct (Reasoning and Acting) loop in action. Conceptually:

System: Today is 2026-06-10
User: What are exciting trips for this summer? I like to go to the beach
Agent: [Reasoning]
Agent: [Act]          [tool call]: searchTrips 2026-06-01 - 2026-08
Agent: [Observe]      [tool response]: [Trip{beginDate=2026-06-14, endDate=2026-06-21, description='7-Night Maldives ... }, ...]
Agent: [Repeat]
Agent: [Reasoning]
Agent: [Finish]       I found an exciting trip for you this summer: ...

The complete loop happens inside the ChatClient. If you want to see the intermediate steps, add a SimpleLoggerAdvisor for logging the model requests and responses.

You can experiment with different queries and see how the agent utilizes the tool to fetch relevant information from our CAP service.

You could also add additional parameters to the tool. For example, you could utilize the description field to search for specific interests like "beach", "skiing", or "hiking". As a simple approach, use a text search. Or use vector embeddings with a cosine similarity search. With CAP, this is quite easy to implement.

Calling the agent in CAP with an action tool (write)

The query tool we implemented is useful to provide the agent with data. However, we can also implement tools that allow an agent to take action. For example, let's implement a tool that allows the agent to book a trip on behalf of the user.

In CAP, a dedicated action allows us to implement the business logic for booking a trip. Earlier, we had already defined the bound action book in our CDS model:

service TravelService {
    @readonly entity Trips as projection on travel.Trips
    actions {
        action book() returns String;
    };
    ...
}

If you still have mvn cds:watch running, the cds model should be recompiled automatically. If not, run mvn compile to compile the model and generate the necessary Java classes for the new action.

Now we implement the corresponding event handler.

// src/main/java/customer/cap_agent/handler/BookingHandler.java
package customer.cap_agent.handler;

import java.util.List;

import org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Autowired;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;

import com.sap.cds.ql.Insert;
import com.sap.cds.ql.cqn.CqnAnalyzer;
import com.sap.cds.services.handler.EventHandler;
import com.sap.cds.services.handler.annotations.On;
import com.sap.cds.services.handler.annotations.ServiceName;
import com.sap.cds.services.persistence.PersistenceService;

import cds.gen.example.travel.Bookings;
import cds.gen.example.travel.Bookings_;
import cds.gen.travelservice.TravelService_;
import cds.gen.travelservice.TripsBookContext;

@Component
@ServiceName(TravelService_.CDS_NAME)
public class BookingHandler implements EventHandler {
  @Autowired
  PersistenceService db;

  @On
  String book(TripsBookContext context) {
    CqnAnalyzer analyzer = CqnAnalyzer.create(context.getModel());
    var result = analyzer.analyze(context.getCqn());
    Integer tripId = (Integer) result.targetKeys().get("ID");

    Bookings booking = Bookings.create();
    booking.setTripId(tripId);
    var query = Insert.into(Bookings_.class).entries(List.of(booking));
    db.run(query);
    return "Successfully booked trip " + tripId;
  }
}

As you see, this is a very simple implementation that just inserts a new booking for the given trip. In a real application, you would usually have a multi-step process that checks for availability with the vendor, handles payment and then comes back with a confirmation. But for demonstration purposes, this implementation is sufficient to show the idea and important considerations.

The action itself is registered to the service and available to the user. However, the bookings entity is only exposed to the user as readonly.

@readonly entity Bookings as projection on travel.Bookings where $user = user;

So in the action implementation, we go directly to the database via the PersistenceService utilizing the db level entity interfaces cds.gen.example.travel.Bookings and cds.gen.example.travel.Bookings_ to insert a new booking. This database insert bypasses the service level permission checks while still maintaining the user context.

Recalling our domain model, we automatically insert the user and the booking date:

entity Bookings {
  key trip        : Association to Trips;
  key user        : User @readonly @(cds.on.insert: $user);
      bookingDate : Date default $now;
}

Now that we have implemented the action, we can expose it as a tool for our agent.

// src/main/java/customer/cap_agent/agent/TravelTools.java
  @Tool
  String bookTrip(
    @ToolParam(description = "ID of the trip") Integer tripId
  ) {
    log.info("[tool call]: bookTrip " + tripId);
    Trips_ trip = CQL.entity(Trips_.class).matching(Map.of("ID", tripId));
    return travelService.book(trip);
  }

We receive the trip id as a parameter from the LLM model and then call the action via travelService.book(trip). This ensures that all the service level logic is applied, including permissions. The trip is an entity reference matching the given ID.

Since our agent now has the ability to book a trip, it is also useful to provide a tool for checking existing bookings, so that the agent can verify if a trip has been booked.

// src/main/java/customer/cap_agent/agent/TravelTools.java
...
import cds.gen.travelservice.Bookings;
import cds.gen.travelservice.Bookings_;
...
  @Tool
  List<Bookings> myBookings() {
    log.info("[tool call]: myBookings");
    var query = Select.from(Bookings_.class);
    var result = travelService.run(query);
    return result.list();
  }

This touches on an important aspect of agent design: providing feedback to the agent about its actions. When an agent takes an action, it should be able to observe the outcome of that action. Not only via the direct response of the action, but also by querying the state of the system.

Now that we have the necessary tools for booking a trip, let's see how our agent utilizes them.

# test/http/TravelService.http
...
### invokeAgent
# @NAME invokeAgent_POST
POST {{server}}/odata/v4/TravelService/invokeAgent
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Basic {{username}}:{{password}}

{
  "input": "Book a trip for me this summer. I like to go to the beach. Don't ask me, just book it."
}

Checking the tool calls, the agent again first searches for available trips in the summer. It then proceeds to book a matching trip. Great!

[tool call]: searchTrips 2026-06-01 - 2026-08-31
[tool call]: bookTrip 2001

Sometimes it might book multiple trips. This highlights the importance of careful agent design and implementation of guardrails. Since agents can take actions autonomously, it is crucial to ensure that they do not take unintended actions that could have negative consequences.

There are multiple dials you can use to improve the agent's behavior, such as adjusting the system prompt, providing more specific instructions in the tool descriptions. Play around with these parameters and see how it affects the agent's behavior.

But despite all instructions, there is still a chance that the agent takes unintended actions. So make sure to also implement guardrails such as confirmation steps for critical actions, or a manual review process for high-impact decisions.

Transactional Handling

CAP Java automatically creates ChangeSet contexts on each request. Since the tool calls are all happening inside a single request, they share the same ChangeSet context and thus the same transaction.

If you have a long running task with multiple modifying tool calls, you have a long running transaction... which is typically a bad thing. To avoid this, we explicitly open a new ChangeSet context inside the booking tool:

// src/main/java/customer/cap_agent/agent/TravelTools.java
  @Autowired
  CdsRuntime runtime;

  @Tool
  String bookTrip(
    @ToolParam(description = "ID of the trip") Integer tripId
  ) {
    log.info("[tool call]: bookTrip " + tripId);
    Trips_ trip = CQL.entity(Trips_.class).matching(Map.of("ID", tripId));
    return runtime.changeSetContext().run(ctx -> {
      return travelService.book(trip);
    });
  }

Now each booking will be handled in its own transaction, which we can observe with logging level com.sap.cds.persistence.sql-tx: DEBUG:

Opened ChangeSet 98
[tool call]: searchTrips 2026-06-01 - 2026-08-31
Creating new transaction with name [PersistenceService$Default-ChangeSet-98]: PROPAGATION_REQUIRES_NEW,ISOLATION_DEFAULT
...
[tool call]: bookTrip 2036
Opened ChangeSet 99
Suspending current transaction, creating new transaction with name [PersistenceService$Default-ChangeSet-99]
...
Completing ChangeSet 99
Initiating transaction commit
...
Resuming suspended transaction after completion of inner transaction
Closed ChangeSet 99
[tool call]: bookTrip 2052
Opened ChangeSet 100
Suspending current transaction, creating new transaction with name [PersistenceService$Default-ChangeSet-100]
...
Completing ChangeSet 100
Initiating transaction commit
...
Resuming suspended transaction after completion of inner transaction
Closed ChangeSet 100
Completing ChangeSet 98
Initiating transaction commit
...
Closed ChangeSet 98

What's next?

We have now seen how to implement an agentic system in CAP Java using the SAP AI SDK and Spring AI by implementing a simple travel agent that can search for trips and book them on behalf of the user.

CAP integrates nicely with the agentic workflow and allows us to easily expose our services as tools for the agent, while also ensuring that all the necessary service level logic and permissions are applied. Since production qualities like authentication, authorization, and database transactions are handled by CAP, we can focus on the domain.

As an experienced CAP developer you may have noticed... tool calls are simply another protocol for calling CAP services. This means we can also implement an adapter to expose a service as a collection of tools. But that's a story for another time.

Depending on your use case, there are a few advanced topics which may become important:

  • Input and Output: In our implementation, we have a single input and output for each session. If you want to have a conversation with multiple turns, you could use Chat Memory to store the conversation history and provide it as context for each turn. Chat memory can also be easily implemented as a CDS model.
  • Structured output: For generating data which can then be given to a technical system, you need to ensure that the output conforms to a specific structure. For models supporting structured output natively, this can be achieved by using AdvisorParams.ENABLE_NATIVE_STRUCTURED_OUTPUT and providing a class. Otherwise Spring AI provides a Structured Output Converter that can convert the output to a specific class, which may need further validation.
  • Multi-agent systems: In this example, we have implemented a single agent that can perform multiple tasks. However, in more complex scenarios, you might want to implement multiple agents that specialize in different tasks and can collaborate with each other. Or you want Joule to be able to call your agent. One option is the Agent2Agent (A2A) protocol, Spring AI also has a blog post on A2A integration. And as with tools, A2A is just another protocol.

But as always, start simple and grow as you go. So go ahead and implement your own agentic system in CAP, and see how it can transform your applications!

if you change depreciation parameters time-dependent on fixed asset level, with a new time-segment starting in fiscal year 2026, then there should be no change in fiscal year 2024. If you recognize a change of planned depreciation values in fiscal year 2024, then this might have another reason as well.

My prior reply was referring to the question about open and closed fiscal years.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 04 - Namespaces, contexts and name prefixes
Baseline date and net due date mismatch in the Import Supplier Invoices app (F3041)
entry.content:
Dear SAP supportteam,

We are facing an issue in the Import Supplier Invoices app (F3041). Invoices imported via the template sometimes show a mismatch between the baseline date and the net due date even on working days that are not bank holidays. We understand and use the Japan feature that adjusts due dates when they fall on bank holidays; however, the shifts we observe occur on non-holiday dates.
In the imported invoice’s Payment tab, the field “Days 1 / In Percent” displays the value “1”. We would like to know:

From which logic or source this “1” is derived and populated during import.
Whether this value is tied to the payment terms definition or defaulted by the app.
Whether this value contributes to the mismatch between the baseline date and the net due 
date.


Our expectation is that the net due date equals the baseline date plus the days defined in 
the payment terms, unless a bank-holiday adjustment applies.

Please help identify why the due date is shifted on non-holiday dates in F3041 and clarify the origin and calculation logic of “

entry.title: Application Decisions for Sales Order Management - Part 3
entry.content:
Summary

In previous deliverables, we examined the use of Sales Order Management capabilities within SAP Customer Experience solutions, including SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) and SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, from both functional/business and technical/architectural perspectives. Those documents explored how customer-facing solutions can support sales engagement, opportunity management, mobility, and omnichannel customer interactions.

You can access these documents here:

Application Decisions for Sales Order Management in CX - Part 1

Application Decisions for Sales Order Management in CX - Part 2

In hybrid architectures, SAP S/4HANA typically remains the system of record for fulfillment and operational execution. While, SAP Customer Experience solutions may manage customer interactions and order capture activities. SAP S/4HANA often orchestrates fulfillment, logistics, billing, financial postings, and downstream operational processes.

This document focuses on SAP S/4HANA Sales as a strategic option for Sales Order Management, describing its capabilities, strengths, limitations, and architectural considerations. The objective is not to position one solution as universally superior, but rather to help architects evaluate the most appropriate target architecture based on operational priorities, customer engagement strategy, user experience expectations, process complexity, and long-term transformation objectives.

Most of the information included in this document is publicly available through SAP resources such as the SAP Help Portal. However, it has been consolidated and summarized here into a single reference document for the convenience of Enterprise Architects and transformation teams.

Architectural Perspective

In many SAP transformation programs, Sales Order Management is no longer evaluated as an isolated application capability, but rather as part of a broader architectural discussion regarding customer engagement, operational integration, process orchestration, and application landscape simplification.

As organizations modernize their application landscapes, Enterprise Architects frequently need to determine whether Sales Order Management should remain primarily ERP-centric within SAP S/4HANA or evolve toward a more customer-centric architecture leveraging SAP Sales Cloud solutions.

This discussion is especially relevant because the decision often impacts not only the sales organization, but also logistics, fulfillment, finance, integration architecture, master data governance, reporting, user experience, and long-term operational complexity.

Note: The purpose of this document is not to provide a comprehensive description of all SAP S/4HANA Sales capabilities or processes. Instead, this article focuses on the capabilities, architectural considerations, and functional areas that are most commonly evaluated when organizations compare or assess SAP S/4HANA Sales against SAP Customer Experience cloud solutions as part of a target architecture definition or transformation initiative.

Solution Options

Depending on the target architecture and business priorities, multiple SAP solutions can support Sales Order Management scenarios. The most common solution options include:

  • SAP S/4HANA Sales
  • SAP Sales Cloud Version 2
  • SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C) – Sales
  • Hybrid architectures combining SAP Sales Cloud solutions with SAP S/4HANA

In hybrid scenarios, SAP Customer Experience solutions are typically responsible for customer engagement, sales execution, opportunity management, and customer-facing activities, while SAP S/4HANA remains responsible for fulfillment orchestration, pricing execution, billing, logistics integration, and financial processing.

As mentioned previously, the primary focus of this article is SAP S/4HANA Sales and its role within modern Sales Order Management architectures.

Characterization of the Solution 

SAP S/4HANA Sales

SAP S/4HANA Sales provides comprehensive Sales Order Management capabilities tightly integrated with operational and enterprise processes such as logistics, warehousing, fulfillment, transportation, billing, finance, and supply chain execution.

One of the most important characteristics of SAP S/4HANA Sales is its ability to support the complete order lifecycle within a unified ERP environment. This enables organizations to maintain transactional consistency while minimizing the need for extensive replication scenarios, synchronization mechanisms, or additional integration layers between customer-facing and operational systems.

SAP S/4HANA Sales supports end-to-end Sales Order Management processes spanning from quotation and order creation through delivery, billing, and revenue recognition. Presales activities can be managed using business documents such as sales inquiries and sales quotations, allowing organizations to support commercial negotiations and customer interactions before the creation of a formal sales order.

Once a customer accepts a quotation and submits a purchase order, the process can trigger the creation of a sales order within SAP S/4HANA. The sales order then becomes the central transactional object driving subsequent operational activities such as availability checks, delivery processing, fulfillment execution, transportation planning, shipping, billing, and financial postings.

This tight integration between commercial and operational processes is one of the primary reasons why many organizations continue to position SAP S/4HANA Sales as the main platform for Sales Order Management. This is particularly common in industries where order processing is highly transactional and deeply connected with supply chain execution, manufacturing processes, inventory management, or complex fulfillment operations.

From a functional perspective, SAP S/4HANA Sales provides mature enterprise-scale capabilities in areas such as complex pricing, advanced Available-to-Promise (ATP), variant configuration, delivery orchestration, credit management, billing integration, and document flow visibility. These capabilities are especially important for organizations operating sophisticated pricing models, configurable product scenarios, high-volume order environments, or strict fulfillment requirements.

Another major advantage of SAP S/4HANA Sales is the native integration with financial and fulfillment processes. By operating within a centralized ERP platform, organizations can reduce replication complexity, centralize master data governance, improve process consistency, and achieve end-to-end operational visibility across the complete order lifecycle.

Additionally, SAP S/4HANA Sales benefits from embedded analytics and enterprise-grade scalability, allowing organizations to support large transaction volumes while maintaining operational transparency and integrated reporting capabilities directly within the ERP environment.

Criteria to determine the most suitable target solution

1. ERP-Centric vs Customer-Centric Strategy

One of the most important architectural decisions during a Sales transformation initiative is determining whether the organization wants to prioritize operational efficiency or customer engagement capabilities as the primary driver for the target solution.

SAP S/4HANA Sales is often a strong fit for organizations where the ERP system already acts as the operational backbone of the enterprise and where sales processes are deeply integrated with fulfillment, logistics, finance, manufacturing, or supply chain execution. In these environments, sales orders are not only commercial documents but also operational triggers that drive downstream business processes. As a result, keeping Sales Order Management directly within SAP S/4HANA can simplify process orchestration, reduce system fragmentation, and improve end-to-end operational visibility.

Organizations that already have ERP-oriented users and operationally focused sales teams may also find SAP S/4HANA Sales to be a natural extension of their existing business processes.

More information available in the Order and Contract Management in the SAP Help Portal.

2. Need for a Single UI

A common requirement observed during transformation programs is the desire to provide sales representatives with a single user interface for managing customer interactions and sales activities. Many organizations view this as an opportunity to simplify the user experience, reduce context switching between systems, and minimize overall application landscape complexity.

In SAP-centric environments, this frequently leads to evaluating whether SAP S/4HANA itself can serve as the primary interface for Sales Order Management activities. By consolidating processes into a single platform, organizations may reduce integration dependencies, simplify master data synchronization, and centralize transactional processing within the ERP environment.

However, while this approach may simplify architecture and operational management, it can also introduce important tradeoffs that should be carefully evaluated. SAP S/4HANA Sales primarily focuses on transactional execution and operational integration rather than customer engagement and sales collaboration.

As a result, organizations may experience limitations related to modern CRM capabilities, mobile-first experiences, customer activity management, guided selling scenarios, or omnichannel customer engagement.

For Enterprise Architects, the challenge is often not simply deciding between one UI or multiple systems, but rather determining whether the architectural simplification gained by consolidating into SAP S/4HANA justifies the potential reduction in customer engagement capabilities.

3. Pricing Complexity

Pricing is frequently one of the most influential factors when defining the target architecture for Sales Order Management. In many industries, pricing logic extends far beyond simple discounts or price lists and instead involves highly sophisticated pricing procedures, condition techniques, rebates, surcharges, customer-specific agreements, contractual pricing, and real-time operational calculations.

SAP S/4HANA Sales has historically been recognized for its mature and highly flexible pricing engine, making it particularly suitable for organizations operating complex pricing models or margin-sensitive business processes. Industries such as manufacturing, wholesale distribution, chemicals, automotive, and industrial products often rely on advanced pricing capabilities tightly integrated with logistics, fulfillment, procurement, or financial processes.

Another important consideration is the requirement for real-time ERP pricing. In many operational environments, pricing calculations depend on live ERP data such as inventory availability, transportation costs, taxes, contractual conditions, or manufacturing constraints. Maintaining pricing execution directly within SAP S/4HANA can therefore simplify consistency, reduce integration dependencies, and avoid discrepancies between front-office and ERP pricing calculations.

For additional information, please refer to the Price Management section in the SAP Help Portal.

4. Integration Complexity

Integration complexity is often a major consideration when evaluating Sales Order Management architectures. Organizations attempting to simplify their application landscapes frequently assess whether maintaining Sales Order Management directly within SAP S/4HANA can reduce the number of systems, interfaces, replication mechanisms, and middleware dependencies required to support end-to-end sales processes.

A primarily SAP S/4HANA-centric architecture can reduce integration overhead by centralizing transactional processing, pricing logic, master data management, and operational execution within a single ERP platform. This may simplify integration monitoring, reduce synchronization challenges, and minimize the need for complex replication scenarios between front-office and back-office applications.

However, hybrid architectures combining SAP Sales Cloud with SAP S/4HANA may still provide important strategic advantages despite the additional integration complexity. These architectures often enable better user experiences, stronger customer engagement capabilities, more flexible sales processes, and improved sales productivity for customer-facing teams.

As a result, the architectural discussion should not focus solely on reducing integrations, but rather on balancing simplification objectives against the business value provided by specialized customer engagement capabilities.

Additional details about integrations within Sales Order Management are available at the following link, including:

  • Integration with External Buyer Systems
  • Integration with SAP Business Network
  • Integration with SAP Global Trade Services
  • Integration with SAP Self-Billing Cockpit
  • Integration with SAP Subscription Billing

5. AI and Intelligent Sales

Artificial Intelligence is becoming an increasingly important factor in Sales transformation initiatives, particularly as organizations look for ways to improve sales productivity, customer engagement, forecasting accuracy, and decision-making capabilities.

SAP S/4HANA tends to focus AI capabilities more toward operational intelligence and ERP-centric processes. Examples include predictive fulfillment scenarios, embedded operational analytics, exception handling, process automation, and ERP-integrated AI use cases designed to improve operational efficiency and transactional visibility across the sales process.

Additionally, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition incorporates Joule, SAP’s generative AI copilot, which provides dedicated use cases for Sales processes. Joule supports users with transactional and operational activities directly within the SAP S/4HANA environment, helping improve productivity and simplify user interactions across sales execution scenarios.

Examples of Joule-supported sales use cases include:

  • Sales order fulfillment monitoring
  • Sales document creation with references
  • Mass changes to sales orders
  • Billing document creation with references
  • Renewing expiring prices
  • Sales quotation updates
  • Displaying lists of open sales documents

In this context, AI within SAP S/4HANA is typically positioned to enhance operational execution, transactional productivity, and enterprise process intelligence, while SAP Sales Cloud solutions remain more focused on customer engagement and sales relationship management scenarios.

For more information about this topic please refer to the Sales section in Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition of the SAP Help Portal site.

6. Offline Requirements

Offline capability remains an important consideration for organizations with mobile sales teams operating in environments where internet connectivity may be inconsistent or unavailable. This requirement is especially common in industries such as consumer products, field sales, distribution, utilities, or remote service operations.

SAP S/4HANA Sales is typically not designed as an offline-first platform. While it provides strong transactional and operational capabilities, its architecture and user experience are generally optimized for connected ERP-centric scenarios rather than disconnected mobile sales operations.

Evaluation Spreadsheet

The following table provides a consolidated comparison of the three primary Sales Order Management solution options discussed across this document and the two previous articles related to SAP Customer Experience solutions. It combines both functional and architectural considerations to help Enterprise Architects evaluate the strengths, limitations, and typical positioning of each option within different transformation scenarios.

The comparison includes capabilities and decision criteria associated with SAP S/4HANA Sales, SAP Sales Cloud Version 2, and SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C), providing a comprehensive overview of the most relevant aspects commonly evaluated during target architecture discussions and Sales transformation initiatives.

 

 Criteria

SAP S/4HANA Sales

SAP Sales Cloud V2

SAP Cloud for Customer (C4C)

Operational Integration

Very Strong

Moderate

Moderate

Logistics & Fulfillment Integration

Very Strong

Dependent on Integration

Dependent on Integration

Customer Engagement

Opportunity Management

Limited
